articles.title: Is there still room for conservative treatment of Distal Biceps Tendon ruptures? What patients (and surgeons) should know. A case series with a mean 4-years follow-up.
articles.authors: Coliva F, Marinelli A, Riva M, Valentina V, Tortorella F, Minerba M, et al.
articles.population: Patients with distal biceps tendon ruptures treated conservatively (non-surgically)
articles.outcomes: Strength deficits (supination and flexion), pain during repetitive activities, DASH and MEPS scores, fatigue
articles.take_home: Non-surgical treatment for distal biceps tendon rupture works reasonably well for sedentary patients, with only modest strength loss and good functional scores. However, people doing heavy or repetitive manual work had worse outcomes and more pain, so surgery may be preferable for them.

articles.title: Propensity Matched Professional Baseball Pitchers With Early Trunk Rotation Demonstrate Increased Shoulder and Elbow Distractive Forces with Equivalent Ball Velocity.
articles.authors: Manzi J, Dillon M, Dowling B, Eskew J, Patel S, Kunze K, et al.
articles.sample_size: 339 pitchers evaluated; 33 with early trunk rotation matched to 132 with late trunk rotation
articles.outcomes: Shoulder and elbow distractive forces, ball velocity, trunk rotation velocity, stride length, shoulder external rotation, pelvic position, and prior injury prevalence
articles.take_home: Professional pitchers who rotate their trunk early (before foot contact) experience greater forces on their shoulder and elbow compared to those who rotate later, but throw at similar speeds. This suggests early trunk rotation may place unnecessary stress on the throwing arm without performance benefit.

articles.title: All-Suture Anchors Exhibit Less Perianchor Fluid Formation Independent of Healing Quality Compared With PEEK Anchors in Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair: A Propensity Score-Matched Comparative Study.
articles.authors: Gul O, Ayazoglu MA, Yazici A, Durusoy S, Gurun E, Okutan AE
articles.study_type: Retrospective comparative study with propensity score matching
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary arthroscopic rotator cuff repair with either all-suture anchors or PEEK anchors
articles.outcomes: Clinical outcomes (pain, shoulder function, range of motion), tendon healing rates, and perianchor fluid collection on MRI at minimum 2-year follow-up
articles.take_home: All-suture anchors and PEEK anchors produced similar clinical results and healing rates, but all-suture anchors had less fluid collection around the anchor. In PEEK anchors, fluid collection was linked to healing problems, but this connection was not seen with all-suture anchors.

articles.title: Towards a common definition of frozen shoulder: a scoping review of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Karasuyama M, Ishikawa H, Uchida T, Saka M, Muraki T, Minamikawa T, et al.
articles.study_type: Scoping review of randomised controlled trials
articles.population: Adults with frozen shoulder or adhesive capsulitis
articles.sample_size: 310 randomised controlled trials included from 4,426 records identified
articles.outcomes: How frozen shoulder has been defined across trials in terms of age criteria, pain characteristics, range of motion limitations, symptom duration, and imaging requirements
articles.take_home: Frozen shoulder is defined very differently across research studies, making it hard to compare results between trials. Researchers need to agree on standard definitions covering age, pain levels, movement restrictions, how long symptoms have lasted, and what imaging tests to use.

articles.title: Temporal trends and estimated lifetime attributable radiation risk of preoperative planning computed tomography for primary shoulder arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Kalva SR, Fucich D, Perry AJ, Lezak B, Torkieh J, Joshi T, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective review of institutional trends and radiation risk analysis
articles.population: Patients undergoing primary shoulder arthroplasty from 2016 to 2024
articles.sample_size: 100 patients stratified by sex, age, and cancer type for risk projection
articles.outcomes: Annual percentage of patients receiving preoperative CT scans; estimated lifetime attributable risk of cancer by age group and sex
articles.take_home: The use of preoperative CT scans for shoulder surgery has increased from 53% to 66% of patients over eight years. These scans carry a small but measurable risk of radiation-related cancer, particularly in younger women, though the overall risk remains low.

articles.title: Shoulder Muscle Adiposity Can Predict the Efficacy of Corticosteroid Injections in Treating Adhesive Capsulitis After Rotator Cuff Repair Surgery.
articles.authors: Chen NH, Lin CL, Cheng YH, Chiu YS, Hsu TH, Huang SW
articles.population: Patients who developed adhesive capsulitis (shoulder stiffness) after rotator cuff repair surgery and received corticosteroid injection
articles.outcomes: Pain scores and range of motion at 8 weeks; fatty infiltration measured on preoperative MRI using Goutallier fatty degeneration index
articles.take_home: Patients with higher levels of fat infiltration in shoulder muscles before surgery (measured on MRI scans) were six times more likely to have corticosteroid injections fail to relieve their stiffness. This finding may help doctors identify which patients are unlikely to benefit from injections so they can consider other treatments earlier.

articles.title: Is a 32 mm glenosphere optimal in reverse shoulder arthroplasty for patients with small glenoid anatomy? : a range of motion simulation study in petite Asian females.
articles.authors: Kiyomatsu H, Fujibuchi T, Kutsuna T, Kinoshita T, Tsuda T, Hayashi M, et al.
articles.population: Asian female patients with small glenoid anatomy undergoing reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.sample_size: 11 patients with postoperative outcomes; 3D CT planning analysis performed
articles.outcomes: Impingement-free range of motion in extension-flexion, abduction-adduction, and external-internal rotation across different glenosphere sizes and inferior overhangs
articles.take_home: In patients with small shoulder bones, using a 32 mm glenosphere with adequate overhang provided better predicted movement range than larger options. However, the researchers note that real-world clinical testing is still needed to confirm these findings account for soft tissues and implant stability.

articles.title: Non-Opioid Pain Management in Younger Patients Undergoing Arthroscopic Glenoid Labrum Surgery: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
articles.authors: Tornberg HN, Czachor MK, Gutowski CT, Criscione JX, Kalani CM, Dibato J, et al.
articles.population: Opioid-naive patients aged 15 to 25 years undergoing arthroscopic glenoid labrum repair
articles.outcomes: Postoperative opioid consumption (morphine milligram equivalents), pain scores (visual analog scale) over 14 days, and patient satisfaction with pain control
articles.take_home: Young patients given a non-opioid pain management plan after shoulder labrum surgery had similar pain relief and satisfaction to those offered opioids, with very few patients in either group actually using opioids. This suggests non-opioid approaches work well for this type of surgery in younger patients.

articles.title: Shoulder Arthroplasty Outcomes in Hypertensive Patients: A Propensity Score-Matched Comparison of ARBs and ACE Inhibitors.
articles.authors: Lee S, Jayne C, Kim MT, Boufadel P, Cook AE, Uppstrom TJ, et al.
articles.study_type: Propensity score-matched cohort study using electronic health records
articles.population: Hypertensive patients undergoing shoulder arthroplasty who took either angiotensin receptor blockers or ACE inhibitors perioperatively
articles.sample_size: 11,603 patients (matching 1:1 between ARB and ACEI groups); including 1,559 anatomic total shoulder arthroplasty, 3,734 reverse shoulder arthroplasty, and 412 hemiarthroplasty patients
articles.outcomes: Dislocation, periprosthetic fracture, stiffness, revision rates, medical complications, surgical complications, readmission, and emergency department visits at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively
articles.take_home: Hypertensive patients taking angiotensin receptor blockers had lower rates of shoulder dislocation, periprosthetic fracture, and stiffness compared to those taking ACE inhibitors after shoulder replacement. These findings suggest angiotensin receptor blockers may offer bone-related benefits, though the advantages were not consistent across all types of shoulder replacement and need further study.

articles.title: Image-Based Quantitative Osteotomy Planning for Congenital Proximal Radioulnar Synostosis: Plan-Execution Discrepancy and Postoperative Radiographic Outcomes.
articles.authors: Chen YC, Shih HJ, Chang CH, Wang TM, Yang CY, Shih PJ
articles.population: Children with congenital proximal radioulnar synostosis (fused bones in forearm)
articles.outcomes: Rotational range of motion, radial head alignment on X-ray, functional outcomes, plan-execution discrepancy
articles.take_home: A new computer-based planning method for forearm surgery showed promise in providing objective surgical guidelines, with all procedures completed safely and seven of eight patients gaining improved rotation. However, the study was small and suggests larger follow-up research is needed to confirm whether this planning approach improves long-term surgical results.

articles.title: Stepwise loss of shoulder stabilizers induces humeral head migration and progression of cuff tear arthropathy in a rat model.
articles.authors: Uekama K, Iuchi T, Ide T, Shimanoe K, Masuda Y, Takada S, et al.
articles.outcomes: Superior humeral head migration, cartilage degeneration, inflammatory markers, bone remodelling changes
articles.take_home: When shoulder stabilizing structures are progressively damaged in this rat model, the humeral head migrates upwards in stages and cartilage degenerates. The superior capsule appears more important than the biceps tendon in preventing this migration, and preservation of shoulder stability may help slow the development of cuff tear arthropathy.

articles.title: Operative Timing Predicts Postoperative Complications after Staged Reverse Total Shoulder Arthroplasty.
articles.authors: Wier J, Mayfield CK, Mouchawar AC, Barcenas AB, Weber AE, Gamradt SC, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using healthcare database
articles.population: Patients in the United States undergoing primary reverse total shoulder arthroplasty between 2016-2020
articles.sample_size: 3,152 staged bilateral procedures matched to 9,456 unilateral procedures
articles.outcomes: 90-day surgical complications including fracture and infection
articles.take_home: Having surgery on the second shoulder within one year of the first reverse shoulder replacement increases the risk of complications compared to having just one shoulder done. Waiting more than 12 months between the two operations reduces this complication risk to levels similar to single-shoulder surgery.

articles.title: Does robust greater tuberosity repair affect the outcomes of reverse shoulder replacement in proximal humerus fractures? - A Bayesian network meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Ponugoti N, Selim A, Elhassan A, Jeavons R
articles.population: Older adults with proximal humerus fractures treated with reverse shoulder arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Constant score (shoulder function measure), shoulder motion (abduction, external rotation, forward flexion)
articles.take_home: Repairing the greater tuberosity bone fragment during reverse shoulder replacement for upper arm fractures led to better shoulder function and movement compared to not repairing it, but only when the repair healed successfully. If the repair did not heal, results were similar to not attempting repair at all.

articles.title: The meta-analysis and systematic review of arthroscopic lower trapezius tendon transfer for massive irreparable rotator cuff tears.
articles.authors: Baek CH, Kim JG, Kim BT, Lim C
articles.population: Patients with massive irreparable posterosuperior rotator cuff tears treated with arthroscopic lower trapezius tendon transfer
articles.outcomes: External rotation improved by 27.9 degrees, forward flexion by 37.0 degrees, pain reduced by 4.2 points on visual analogue scale, ASES score improved by 32.5 points, Constant score improved by 22.7 points, graft healing 89.4%, complications 3.70%, reoperation 3.46%, conversion to reverse shoulder replacement 2.08%
articles.take_home: Arthroscopic lower trapezius tendon transfer successfully improved shoulder movement, reduced pain and improved function in patients with massive irreparable rotator cuff tears, with good healing rates and low complication rates.
